overview: The Gemara discusses the laws of acquisition when one rides an animal and another leads it, analyzing the status of the rider (רכוב) versus the leader (מנהיג) and the concept of 'מוסירה'. — discussion continues into next amud.

sugyaBoundaries.gist: The Gemara discusses the laws of acquisition when one rides an animal and another leads it, analyzing the status of the rider (רכוב) versus the leader (מנהיג) and the concept of 'מוסירה'. — discussion continues into next amud.

steps.title: Clarifying the scope of the uncertainty
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks whether the uncertainty applies to a rider alone and a leader alone, or to a rider in the presence of a leader.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא narrows the scope of רַב יְהוּדָה's uncertainty. It clarifies that the doubt is specifically about a rider when a leader is also present.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא rejects the idea that the doubt concerns a rider alone or a leader alone, as both clearly acquire in isolation. The real question is whether the rider's grasp or the leader's control takes precedence when both are present.

keyTerms.meaning: rider in the presence of a leader

phrases.aramaic: הֵיכִי דָמֵי? אִילֵּימָא רָכוּב לְחוֹדֵיהּ וּמַנְהִיג לְחוֹדֵיהּ –
phrases.english: What are the circumstances?

phrases.aramaic: מַנְהִיג לְחוֹדֵיהּ מִי אִיכָּא מַאן דְּאָמַר לָא קָנֵי?
phrases.english: Is there anyone who says that one who leads an animal alone does not acquire it?

phrases.aramaic: אֶלָּא אִי אִיכָּא לְמֵימַר דְּלָא קָנֵי –
phrases.english: Rather, if there is a case where it could be said that one does not acquire the…
It is obviously in the case of one who sits in a riding position that…

phrases.aramaic: אֶלָּא רָכוּב בִּמְקוֹם מַנְהִיג אִיבַּעְיָא לֵיהּ.
phrases.english: Rather, his dilemma was with regard to a case where one sits in a riding…
What is the halakha?
as the animal is in his grasp

phrases.aramaic: אוֹ דִלְמָא מַנְהִיג עָדִיף – דְּאָזְלָא מֵחֲמָתֵיהּ?
phrases.english: or perhaps the one leading the animal takes precedence, as it walks because of him?

steps.triggerLanguage: היכא דמי? אילימא רכוב לחודיה ומנהיג לחודיה

rashi.hebrew: אי נימא רכוב לחודיה - שאין אחר מנהיג עמו ובא אחד מן השוק וחוטפו ממנו בפנינו ומושכה לקנותה וכן מנהיג ואין רכוב עמו ובא אחד וחוטפו ממנו בפנינו לקנותה וקאמר שמואל בחד מינייהו דלא קני ומאן דחטף שפיר חטף ומבעיא ליה לרב יהודה בהי מינייהו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara initially considers if the question is about a rider alone or a leader alone, where a third party snatches the animal, and whether either of these acts constitutes acquisition.

rashi.hebrew: מנהיג לחודיה מי איכא למאן דאמר דלא קני - הא משיכה היא זו ובהמה נקנית במשיכה ומאי תיבעי ליה לרב יהודה אי ודאי אמר שמואל בחד מינייהו דלא קני ברכוב הוא דקאמר דקסבר משיכה בעינן שתעקור יד ורגל כדאמרינן בקדושין (דף כב:):
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that leading alone is universally accepted as a valid משיכה for acquisition, so the uncertainty must specifically concern the rider, who might not acquire if משיכה requires the animal to uproot its limbs.

rashi.hebrew: אלא רכוב במקום מנהיג - שניהם באים לפנינו זה רכוב וזה מנהיג זה אומר כולה שלי וזה אומר כולה שלי אמר שמואל חד קני ומבעיא ליה לרב יהודה היכא דאיתנהו לתרוייהו הי עדיף רכוב עדיף דהא תפיס בה וכי אמרינן משיכה קני היכא דליכא רכוב אבל במקום רכוב לא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י defines the scenario as two claimants appearing before the court, one riding and one leading, and explains that the rider's advantage is his תפיסה, which might be sufficient for acquisition only when no one else is riding.

rashi.hebrew: או דלמא מנהיג עדיף דקאזלא מחמתיה - והיא הגדולה בקנין:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the leader's advantage is that the animal moves because of him, which constitutes the greater form of acquisition.

tosafot.hebrew: מנהיג לחודיה מי איכא מאן דאמר לא קנה - אע"ג דלר"ש אית ליה בפ"ק דקדושין (דף כה: ושם) דבהמה [בין דקה בין גסה] אינה נקנית אלא בהגבהה מ"מ פריך מכח סתם מתניתין דהא תנן אחד רכוב ואחד מנהיג כו' והא דתנן בפ"ק דקדושין (שם) בהמה גסה נקנית במסירה דברי ר"מ ור"א אף במסירה קאמר וכ"ש במשיכה וכן התם בגמ' דהיכא דמזכיר מסירה הוי פי' אף במסירה ובמקום שמזכיר משיכה דוקא ולא מסירה ובלאו הכי צריך לומר כן התם:
tosafot.english: [Concerning one who] solely leads [the animal], is there anyone who says that he does not acquire [the animal]? The Gemara at this point is considering whether Shmuel’s question was whether leading an animal or riding it is an act of acquisition. The Gemara argues that if we are speaking of leading alone or riding alone, there can be no question that leading alone is an act of acquisition. That this is so apparently clear to the Gemara indicates that nobody can possibly argue with this. Tosfos finds that R’ Shimon (Kiddushin 25b) does in fact disagree. If so, we must understand the basis for our Gemara’s assumption that leading an animal is definitely a method of acquisition. Even though R’ Shimon in a Baraisa in the first Perek of Kiddushin (25b) maintains that [both small and large] animals can only be acquired by lifting them, which implies that leading them is not legally recognized as a method of acquisition, even so, [the Gemara’s] assertion that one must hold leading an animal is a method of acquisition, is based on our anonymous Mishna which teaches: Or if one was riding and one was leading etc. [an animal, thus demonstrating ownership, they divide the animal]. Our Mishna certainly indicates that leading an animal is a valid method of acquisition. Shmuel would definitely not issue a ruling that does not conform to Mishna (2a), an anonymous Mishna, even though R’ Shimon in Kiddushin (25b) does not agree with Mishna (2a). If the strength of the Gemara’s argument is the anonymous Mishna (2a), it follows that all other anonymous Mishnayos agree with it, but Tosfos introduces an anonymous Mishna in Kiddushin (25b) which also seems to say that leading an animal is not a proper method of acquisition? Let us study that Mishna together. A large animal can be acquired by “handing over” and a small animal by lifting it. These are the words of R’ Meir and R’ Eliezer. The Rabanan say: a small animal is acquired by leading it. An analysis of the text yields that the Rabanan hold that only a small animal is acquired by leading it. Insofar as a large animal is concerned they seem to agree that “handing over” is the method of acquisition. The opinion of the Rabanan, who are the majority, has the strength of an anonymous Mishna. Their opinion seems to contradict Mishna (2a) which says that even a large animal is acquired by leading it? If so, why does the Gemara argue that Shmuel must hold that a large animal is acquired by leading it, since anonymous Mishna (2a) is of that opinion, perhaps Shmuel rules in accordance with the Mishna in Kiddushin (25b) which says that a large animal is acquired by “handing over”? That which we learned in a Mishna in the first Perek of Kiddushin (25b): A large animal can be acquired by “handing it over” and a small animal by lifting it, these are the words of R’ Meir and R’ Eliezer?1See מהרש"א who explains that Tosfos was a bit brief, where they should have explained their question in a lengthier manner. The strict reading of Tosfos seems to indicate that Tosfos is asking his question from the opinions of R’ Meir and R’ Eliezer. This however cannot be the case, because their opinion does not have the force of an anonymous Mishna. The question Tosfos is asking is based on the majority opinion of the Rabanan, whose opinion has the force of an anonymous Mishna. The Rabanan disagree about a small animal and say that leading it is the method for a small animal. They do not disagree about a large animal. It seems that the Rabanan are also of the opinion that “handing over” is a valid acquisition for acquiring a large animal. The opinion of the Rabanan, who are the majority, has the same force as an anonymous Mishna. If so, why is the Gemara so certain that Shmuel must rule that “leading” is a method of acquisition for a large animal, because Mishna (2a) says so? Perhaps Shmuel rules in accordance with the Rabanan of Kiddushin (25b), which is equal to an anonymous Mishna? Tosfos explains: when R’ Meir and R’ Eliezer speak of “handing over” they mean that it is also a method of acquisition, and certainly leading it is an acquisition. The Rabanan who agree with them insofar as a large animal is concerned are agreeing that “handing over” is also a method of acquisition. They are not saying that “leading” is not an acquisition. Thus, there is no contradiction between anonymous Mishna (2a) and the Rabanan of Kiddushin (25b). So too, when the Gemara there mentions that “handing over” is a method of acquisition, the meaning is that “handing over” is also a method of acquisition. But in such places where [the Gemara] mentions that leading is an acquisition, it means only leading and not “handing over”. In order to reconcile the Gemara’s argument that all must agree leading is an acquisition, with Mishna Kiddushin (25b), Tosfos resorts to an unorthodox interpretation of said Mishna. The Mishna which reads “handing over” is a method of acquisition, must mean that it is also a method of acquisition. Usually, one does not resort to such a novel interpretation without some proof that this is in fact the intent of that Mishna. Tosfos therefore says: Aside from this problem with the Gemara here, which indicates that all must agree that leading an animal is a valid acquisition, it is necessary to say so2See Tosfos there ד'ה בהמה גסה נקנית במסירה where Tosfos discusses at length the need for interpreting the Mishna (Kiddushin 25b) as meaning “handing over” is also an acquisition. in order to understand the Gemara in Kiddushin (25b).
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות addresses the assumption that leading alone is a valid acquisition by noting that although ר' שמעון in קידושין holds animals are only acquired by הגבהה, the anonymous משנה in our tractate clearly validates leading, and שמואל would not contradict a סתם משנה.

tosafot.hebrew: רכוב הוא דלא קני - ומתני' דאחד רכוב דחי לקמן במנהיג ברגליו:
tosafot.english: [It can be said only about the] rider, that he does not acquire [the animal he is riding]. The Gemara is arguing that leading is definitely a valid method of acquisition, since our specifically mentions leading. It is only riding that might not be a method of acquisition. But our Mishna also speaks of riding, just as it speaks of leading? If leading is an acquisition, shouldn’t riding also be an acquisition? The Mishna (2a) which also speaks of one who is riding in the same context as one who is leading, seems to indicate that riding is also an acquisition. [The Gemara] later, deflects an attempt to prove from the Mishna that riding is a valid acquisition by saying that the Mishna is speaking of one who is leading the animal by causing it to move with his feet. He is not merely sitting on the animal. The Gemara’s discussion of riding is limited to one who is sitting on the animal and not causing it to move at all.
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that the Gemara assumes riding alone might not be a valid acquisition, and anticipates that the later resolution will clarify that the משנה refers to a rider who also leads with his feet, not one who merely sits.

tosafot.hebrew: רכוב עדיף דתפיס בה - אין לפרש דתפיס ברגליו או במה שיושב עליה דהאי לא מיקרי תפיס בה אלא במה שהוא תפוס במוסירה קרי תפיס בה דהא בסמוך נקט האי לישנא וצריך לומר דתפיס בה ואזלא נמי מעט מחמתיה או דלמא מנהיג עדיף דאזלא מחמתיה טובא דאי לא מיבעיא ליה ברכוב אלא משום דתפיס בה גרידא א"כ מה פושט בסמוך מיושב דפטור והכי נמי דרכוב לא קני והלא לענין כלאים אינו תלוי כלל בתפיסה אלא באזלא מחמתיה ולענין קנין אינו תלוי כי אם בתפיס בה ולא באזלא מחמתיה:
tosafot.english: [Does the] rider [have a] superior [right to the animal] since he holds it? Tosfos searches for a definition of “he holds it”. Which specific action is called “holding it”? In the second section Tosfos will also discuss whether holding it alone is the determinant factor for acquiring the animal. We should not explain that [a rider] “holds” [an animal] with his legs wrapped around the midsection or by sitting on it, for these are not called “holding it”.1Wrapping one’s legs around the midsection of an animal or sitting on it are ways of benefiting from the animal, but they do not exhibit control. Holding the bridle is a way of exhibiting control. Rather, the fact that he is holding the bridle is called “he holds it”. The proof of this contention is that [the Gemara] will presently use this expression - he is holding the bridle. Tosfos has explained the physical action of “holding it”, but we still need to know the logic behind this method of acquisition. We must say that together with the feature that he is holding [the bridle], [the animal] is also moving slightly because of him and this combination of features is why holding the bridle is a method of acquisition. But if it is an acquisition because he moves the animal slightly, why does the Gemara suggest that leading might possibly be superior because the leader moves it ? The rider also moves the animal. Tosfos explains the second side of the Gemara’s query: Or perhaps a leader is superior because the animal moves much more because of [a leader] as opposed to a rider. Tosfos tells us the reason why he is compelled to say that the movement of the animal is also an important factor in the acquisition of a rider and it is not only because he is holding the bridle. The Gemara will soon compare the rules of acquiring an animal to the rules governing liability for drawing a wagon with an illegal combination - an ox and a donkey. Tosfos draws his conclusion from the basis of this comparison. For if [the Gemara’s] question about whether riding is a valid method of acquisition is only because [the rider] is holding [the animal] by the bridle, and that alone is the reason that he acquires it, then how can [the Gemara] presently resolve this question by comparing holding to one who sits in a wagon which is drawn by an illegal combination and is exempt from receiving lashes because he has not caused the wagon to move, and conclude that so too, a rider who is holding the animal cannot acquire it? The comparison of the two cases is totally unfair. For in regard to the prohibition of drawing a wagon with an illegal combination of animals is concerned, violation does not all depend on whether he is holding the animals, but rather on whether they move the wagon because of him. Whereas, insofar as acquiring is concerned, the validity of the act is dependent only on whether he is holding [the animal] and not on whether it moves because of him? Since the Gemara does compare the two cases, the basis of comparison must be whether one who rides an animal or sits in a wagon is causing it to move. Thus, Tosfos has effectively demonstrated that acquiring an animal is also dependent not on the holding factor alone but on whether he is also causing it to move.
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות defines תפיסה not as merely sitting or wrapping legs, but as holding the bridle, and argues that for the rider to have any claim, he must also be causing the animal to move slightly, otherwise the comparison to the leader's movement would be irrelevant.

tosafot.hebrew: או דלמא מנהיג עדיף כו' - תימה דלפשוט ממתני' דמנהיג קונה במקום רכוב ואי רכוב איירי במנהיג ברגליו כ"ש דמנהיג עדיף דאפילו במקום רכוב ומנהיג ברגליו קני כ"ש במקום רכוב לחודיה וי"ל דמן המשנה אין להוכיח דקני מנהיג במקום רכוב דאפילו לא קני מיקרי מוחזק דכיון דמנהיג לחודיה שלא במקום רכוב קני מקרי מוחזק אפי' במקום רכוב ומ"מ לקמן מוכיח שפיר ממתניתין דרכוב לחודיה קני מאחר שקרוי מוחזק דאי אפילו לחודיה לא קני סברא הוא דאפי' מוחזק נמי לא הוי וכן לעיל דקאמר אילימא מהא שנים אוחזין האי אמר כולה אגבהתי' אבל שנים שהגביהו לא קנו התם נמי אע"ג דכמו שהם שניהם תפוסים לא קנו מקרי מוחזק כיון דאם היה מגביה לחודיה אילו לא היה חבירו תפוס בצד השני היה קונה לכך איקרי מוחזק אפילו עם חבירו ומסיפא אין להוכיח דרכוב ומנהיג קנו שניהם זה במקום זה דקתני אם היו שניהם מודים יחלוקו דאין שניהם מודים שכך קנו אותה ביחד זה ברכיבה וזה בהנהגה אלא שניהם בהנהגה או בקנין אחר:
tosafot.english: Or perhaps a leader is superior [since it moves because of him]. The Gemara assumed that Shmuel was speaking of when one of the litigants was riding the animal and the other was leading it. Shmuel says that one of them acquires the animal and the other does not. Rav Yehudah could not remember which of the two is the superior acquisition. The Gemara is now explaining the various attributes of either acquisition. It now says: Perhaps leading is a superior acquisition since he causes the animal to move? It emerges from the very question, that we could conceivably decide that a leader is not a superior acquisition and it is ineffective when the opponent is riding the animal. Tosfos finds this bewildering! How is it possible to consider the possibility that leading is not effective when the opponent is riding the animal? Doesn’t our Mishna clearly say otherwise? Tosfos is perplexed because he assumes that when Mishna (2a) speaks of one litigant riding the animal and the other leading, the Mishna means that these were the methods of acquisition. If so, the Mishna is a clear indication that leading an animal is an effective acquisition even when one’s opponent is riding it. This is bewildering! For we should conclude from our Mishna (2a) that a leader acquires in the presence of an opponent who rides? It is certainly evident from the Mishna that a leader acquires when one is leading and the other is riding. It is not clear that a rider acquires when his opponent is leading. Even though the Mishna does say that the rider receives half, the Mishna may be speaking of when the rider is also driving the animal with his feet as riders often do. Shmuel’s query about a rider is only when he is not actively making an effort to move the animal, as we will soon see in the Gemara. Tosfos wishes to point out that even according to the Gemara that soon says the Mishna is speaking of a rider who is moving the animal with his legs, there is still proof that a leader alone acquires. For even if the rider [the Mishna] speaks of is driving with his feet as the Gemara soon says, even so, a leader is certainly superior to only riding. For if even when the rider is driving with his feet, [the leader] acquires, certainly he acquires when the rider is only riding. In order to answer this question, Tosfos must retract his original assumption that when the Mishna speaks of a rider versus a leader, it is discussing the method of acquiring the animal. We must distinguish between actually acquiring an animal and demonstrating possession of said animal. Any method of acquisition, even if not effective in a particular situation is still considered to be a demonstration of possession as we will now see. b> We can answer: From the Mishna we cannot prove that a leader acquires in the presence of a rider. For even if [a leader] cannot acquire in the presence of a rider, he is considered to be demonstrating possession. For since leading alone, when not in the presence of a rider acquires, he is considered to be showing possession even in the presence of a rider. But later the Gemara attempts to prove from this very case in the Mishna that riding is an effective method of acquiring? It seems that the Mishna equates a demonstration of possession with an effective acquisition. Even so, later [the Gemara] correctly proves from our Mishna that riding alone acquires, since it is considered a demonstration of possession. For if even [riding] alone does not acquire, logic dictates that it is not a demonstration of possession in the presence of leading. Tosfos needs to show that his approach to differentiating between that which is an effective acquisition and that which is a demonstration of possession is evident from an earlier Gemara. He turns to 8a, where the Gemara discusses Rami bar Chama’s ruling that one can lift a found object for his fellow. The Gemara says: From where in Mishna (2a) did Rami bar Chama deduce that one may acquire a found object for his fellow? If we should say: From the first case: Two are grasping a cloak with each one claiming it is mine. We rule that they share the cloak. We are assuming that the ruling is based on the fact that since they are both exhibiting the way they acquired the cloak, they divide it. This shows that when they both lift it together they acquire it for each other. The Gemara rejects this proof: There, in the first case of the Mishna, this one says it is all mine and I lifted it entirely, and this one says it is all mine and I lifted it entirely. Neither claims that they lifted the cloak together, rather each of them says I lifted the cloak myself and it is all mine. Our ruling that they divide the cloak is not because we believe that they lifted the cloak simultaneously. Rather, it is because we do not know the truth and we must therefore divide it. This does not in any way prove that when they lift together they acquire it. We see from this answer that even though the way they are presently holding the cloak could not have been a way of acquiring it, even so, it is a proper demonstration of possession. So too, earlier (8a), the Gemara says: If you say that Rami bar Chama’s proof is from this case: two are grasping a cloak and we rule that they divide it. It seems that when they lifted the cloak simultaneously they both acquired it. This demonstrates that one can lift a found object for his fellow. The Gemara rejects this argument: This one says: I lifted the entire cloak and they divide because we cannot determine who the true owner is, but two who lift a cloak together would not acquire it at all. There, as well we see, that even though as they are both holding the cloak they could not acquire it, since one cannot lift a found object for his fellow, they are considered demonstrating possession, since if each of them lifted it alone, had his fellow not been lifting it on the other side, he could acquire it, he is therefore considered demonstrating possession even as he holds it together with his fellow. There is a final case in the Mishna (2a) that we might believe proves that both a leader and a rider can acquire in the presence of each other. Tosfos shows us that this is a fallacy. From the last case of the Mishna we can also not prove that a rider and a leader can acquire in each other’s presence. Why might we think that the final case of the Mishna proves this point? For [the Mishna] teaches: If both [litigants] admit, they divide the cloak and there is no need for an oath. What are they admitting? A superficial reading of the Mishna might indicate that they both acquired the animal as they are presently exhibiting ownership, one by riding the animal and the other by leading it. This seems to prove that had they acquired it in this manner, they would both be declared owners. Tosfos says that this is not so. For they are not both admitting that this is how they acquired the animal together, this one by riding and this one by leading, rather, they are admitting that both acquired the animal by leading it together or through some other method of acquisition. That one is riding the animal and the other leading it is merely their way of demonstrating possession.
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות questions why the Gemara considers the possibility that the leader does not acquire when the opponent is riding, given the משנה, and answers that the משנה only establishes the leader as a מוחזק because he acquires when alone, not necessarily that he acquires in the presence of a rider.

meforshim.interplaySummary: תוספות challenges the Gemara's assumption that leading alone is a valid acquisition by citing ר' שמעון in קידושין, but resolves it by appealing to the authority of the סתם משנה in our tractate, whereas רש״י simply accepts the Gemara's premise that leading is a clear משיכה.

steps.title: Rav Yosef's proof from the Mishnah about Kilayim
steps.stepSummary: Rav Yosef cites a Mishnah about Kilayim to prove that a rider does not acquire.
steps.whatsHappening: רַב יוֹסֵף brings a proof from a מִשְׁנָה in קִלְאֵי הַכֶּרֶם to resolve the uncertainty. He argues that since a person in a wagon does not acquire, a rider should also not acquire.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The מִשְׁנָה states that one sitting in a wagon is liable for קִלְאֵי הַכֶּרֶם, but רַבִּי מֵאִר exempts him. Since שְׁמוּאֵל follows the רַבָּנִים who exempt the wagon-sitter due to lack of action, a rider (who also lacks action) should not acquire.

phrases.english: Rav Yosef said:
Rav Yehuda said to me:
Let us see ourselves, as we learned:
The one who leads takes the forty lashes,
and the one sitting in the wagon takes the forty lashes.

phrases.aramaic: רַבִּי מֵאִיר פּוֹטֵר אֶת הַיּוֹשֵׁב בַּקָּרוֹן.
phrases.english: Rabbi Meir exempts the one sitting in the wagon.
And from the fact that Shmuel reversed and taught:
And the Rabbis exempt the one sitting in the wagon,

phrases.aramaic: שְׁמַע מִינַּהּ רָכוּב לְחוֹדֵיהּ לָא קָנֵי,
phrases.english: Learn from it that one who rides alone does not acquire,
and all the more so one who rides in the place of the leader.

steps.triggerLanguage: אמר רב יוסף, אמר לי רב יהודה: נחזי אנן, דתנן: המנהיג סופג את הארבעים

rashi.hebrew: נחזי אנן - אית לן למפשט משמעתא דמר שמואל בהי מינייהו אמר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that נחזי אנן means we can resolve the uncertainty about שמואל's opinion by examining the Mishnah.

rashi.hebrew: המנהיג - בשור וחמור:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that המנהיג refers to leading an ox and a donkey.

rashi.hebrew: פוטר - קסבר לאו מידי עביד:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבי מאיר exempts the one sitting in the wagon because he performs no action.

rashi.hebrew: ומדאפיך שמואל כו' - ש"מ סבירא לשמואל רכוב לאו מידי מעשה עביד ומשום דיחיד ורבים הלכה כרבים אפכה למתניתין לאוקמא פטורא כרבנן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that since שמואל holds like the רבים, he must agree that a rider performs no action, and therefore does not acquire.

rashi.hebrew: הכי גרסינן שמע מינה ס"ל לשמואל רכוב לחודיה לא קני - ולא גרס לרבנן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י notes that the correct text reads שמע מינה ס

tosafot.hebrew: רכוב לחודיה לא קני - נראה דרב יהודה היה מסופק אם שמע משמואל ברכוב לחודיה ומנהיג לחודיה ואז פשיט ליה דרכוב הוא דלא קני או אם שמיע ליה ברכוב במקום מנהיג ואז מספקא ליה הי מינייהו קני דאי לא מספקא ליה אלא שמע משמואל ברכוב במקום מנהיג ותו לא א"כ ס"ל לשמואל דרכוב לחודיה קני והשתא פשיט רב יהודה דאפילו לחודיה לא קני:
tosafot.english: Riding alone is not an acquisition. At this point in the dialogue the Gemara concludes that riding alone is not a valid method of acquisition. This seems to contradict the simple reading of the Gemara earlier. Let us review the Gemara: The Gemara relates that Shmuel said: Concerning riding and leading, one is a valid method of acquisition and the other is not. Rav Yehudah commented: I do not know which one is the valid method of acquisition. A) The Gemara wondered about Rav Yehudah’s query whether leading or riding is a valid method of acquisition. Was Shmuel possibly speaking of leading alone or riding alone? The Gemara argued that this could not have been the question, because leading alone is definitely a method of acquisition. It is certain that if only one of the two is a method of acquisition and not the other, it must be that leading is an acquisition and riding is not. B) The Gemara then says: rather, the question was whether riding is a method of acquisition in the presence of a leader? This conclusion indicates that certainly riding alone is an acquisition. Shmuel’s statement was only about riding versus leading. C) The Gemara then present a Mishna (Kilayim 8, 3) as proof that even riding alone is not an acquisition. This understanding of the Gemara, although logical creates a major problem in the flow of the Gemara. For initially the Gemara in step b) was certain that riding alone is an acquisition and then in step c) it concludes that even riding alone is not a method of acquisition. The Gemara does not usually categorically say that the halacha is one way and then reverse itself and say the halacha is the opposite. We must find a way of reading the Gemara so that the question of whether riding alone is an acquisition remained open and would then be subject to a conclusion that it is not an acquisition. Tosfos re-defines Rav Yehudah’s query about Shmuel’s statement: It appears that Rav Yehudah was in doubt if the statement he heard from Shmuel was about riding alone and leading alone, and then, it would be plain to him that Shmuel said riding was not an acquisition, because leading alone is known to be an acquisition. Or, if he heard from [Shmuel] about riding in the presence of a leader, and in that case, he is in doubt which of the two is an acquisition. According to this interpretation, the Gemara in step b) never concluded that riding is an acquisition. The Gemara simply said that if Shmuel’s statement was about riding alone and leading alone, it would be certain that leading alone was an acquisition and riding was not. The Gemara never definitively stated that this was Shmuel’s position. The question remained open. The Gemara can therefore conclude in step c) that even riding alone is not a method of acquisition. For if [Rav Yehudah] ultimately had no doubt about riding alone, as we explained in the simple reading of the Gemara, rather, he definitely heard from Shmuel that his statement was only about riding in the presence of a leader, if so, the Gemara has initially concluded in step b) that Shmuel maintains that riding alone is an acquisition, and now in step c) the Gemara resolves that even riding alone is not an acquisition. The Gemara does not change its conclusions. We must say that in step b) the question of riding alone did remain open. It is then fair to say that in step c) the Gemara concludes it is not an acquisition.
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that רב יהודה was uncertain whether שמואל's statement referred to riding/leading alone or riding in the presence of a leader, and the Gemara's proof from the Mishnah resolves this by showing that even riding alone does not constitute acquisition.

steps.title: Abaye's challenge to Rav Yosef's attribution
steps.stepSummary: Abaye challenges Rav Yosef's attribution of the proof to Rav Yehuda.
steps.whatsHappening: אֲבָיֵי challenges why רַב יוֹסֵף attributes this proof to רַב יְהוּדָה when he has used it before without attribution. This questions the reliability of the attribution.
steps.deeperAnalysis: אֲבָיֵי points out that רַב יוֹסֵף has used this proof many times without attributing it to רַב יְהוּדָה. This suggests the proof is רַב יוֹסֵף's own reasoning, not רַב יְהוּדָה's.

phrases.english: Abaye said to Rav Yosef:

phrases.aramaic: הָא זִמְנִין סַגִּיאִין אֲמַרְתְּ לַן: נֶחְזֵי אֲנַן,
phrases.english: Many times you said to us: Let us see ourselves,

phrases.aramaic: וְלָא אֲמַרְתְּ לַן מִשְּׁמֵיהּ דְּרַב יְהוּדָה.
phrases.english: and you did not say to us in the name of Rav Yehuda.

steps.triggerLanguage: אמר ליה אבא לרב יוסף: הא זמנין סגיאין אמרת לן: נחזי אנן

rashi.hebrew: והא זמנין סגיאין אמרת - להא שמעתין נחזי אנן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the phrase נחזי אנן refers to the specific proof being discussed, which Rav Yosef has previously presented as his own reasoning.

rashi.hebrew: ולא אמרת לן - אלא משמך למפשט ספיקו דרב יהודה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that Rav Yosef previously used this proof to resolve Rav Yehuda's doubt without attributing it to Rav Yehuda, implying it was Rav Yosef's own derivation.

steps.title: Rav Yosef's defense of his attribution
steps.stepSummary: Rav Yosef defends his attribution by explaining that he remembers Rav Yehuda making the proof and also remembers his own objection to it.
steps.whatsHappening: רַב יוֹסֵף defends his attribution by recalling the full exchange with רַב יְהוּדָה. He explains that רַב יְהוּדָה made the proof, and he objected that a rider holds the reins, which is a mode of acquisition.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב יוֹסֵף recalls that רַב יְהוּדָה responded that רַב and שְׁמוּאֵל both hold that holding the reins does not effect acquisition. This validates the proof and introduces מוּסִירָה as a potential mode of acquisition.

phrases.english: He said to him: Indeed!
And I also remember that I said to him:
How does the Master derive [the law of] one riding from one sitting?

phrases.aramaic: יוֹשֵׁב לָא תָּפֵיס בְּמוֹסֵירָה, רָכוּב תָּפֵיס בְּמוֹסֵירָה!
phrases.english: One sitting does not grasp the reins, one riding grasps the reins!

phrases.aramaic: וַאֲמַר לִי רַב וּשְׁמוּאֵל דְּאָמְרִי תַּרְוַיְיהוּ:
phrases.english: And Rav and Shmuel said to me, both of them say:
Grasping the reins does not acquire.

steps.triggerLanguage: אמר ליה: אברא! ודכירנא נמי דאמרי ליה: היכי פשיט מר רכוב מיושב?

rashi.hebrew: אברא - אמת הוא אמר לי נחזי אנן וזכור אני שאמרתי לו היכי תפשוט מר רכוב מיושב בקרון:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that אברא means Rav Yosef is confirming that Rav Yehuda indeed said נחזי אנן, and that Rav Yosef remembers raising the objection about רכוב versus יושב.

rashi.hebrew: מוסירה אינה קונה - במציאה עד שיוליכנה ותעקור יד ורגל ואף על גב דבמכירה קני כדתנן (קדושין דף כה:) בהמה גסה נקנית במסירה לגבי מציאה לא קני כדאמרינן טעמא לקמן בשמעתין:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that מוסירה does not acquire an unowned item (מציאה) because it requires the item to be uprooted and moved, unlike in a sale where מוסירה is sufficient.

steps.title: Alternative version of Abaye's challenge
steps.stepSummary: An alternative version of Abaye's challenge is presented, where Rav Yosef responds with a different source.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא presents an alternative version of the exchange between אֲבָיֵי and רַב יוֹסֵף. In this version, רַב יוֹסֵף responds that אִידִי taught in a בְּרֵיתָא that holding the reins does not effect acquisition.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This version uses a בְּרֵיתָא from אִידִי instead of רַב and שְׁמוּאֵל. It provides an alternative source for the same halachic principle.

phrases.aramaic: אִיכָּא דְאָמְרִי, אֲמַר לֵיהּ אַבָּיֵי לְרַב יוֹסֵף:
phrases.english: There are those who say, Abaye said to Rav Yosef:
How does the Master resolve the case of one riding from one sitting?

phrases.aramaic: יוֹשֵׁב לָא תָּפֵיס בְּמוֹסֵירָה, רָכוּב תָּפֵיס בְּמוֹסֵירָה!
phrases.english: One sitting does not grasp the reins, one riding grasps the reins!

phrases.aramaic: אֲמַר לֵיהּ, הָכִי תְּנָא אִידֵּי: מוֹסֵירָה לָא קָנֵי.
phrases.english: He said to him, thus he taught at another time: Grasping the reins does not acquire.

steps.triggerLanguage: איכא דאמרי, אמר ליה אבא לרב יוסף: היכי פשיט מר רכוב מיושב?

rashi.hebrew: איכא דאמרי - רב יוסף משמיה דנפשיה קאמר נחזי אנן וא"ל אביי היכי תפשוט רכוב כו':
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that in this alternative version, רב יוסף is stating his own reasoning (נחזי אנן) rather than citing a previous authority, and אביי challenges him on how he can derive a ruling for a rider from a case of a seated person.

rashi.hebrew: אידי - שם חכם:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that אידי is the name of a sage who taught the ברייתא.

steps.title: Rav Huna's statement about מוסירה
steps.stepSummary: Rav Huna states that holding the reins acquires from a person but not from a found object or a convert's property.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא cites רַב הוּנָא's statement about the limits of acquisition by מוּסִירָה. He distinguishes between acquiring from a person and acquiring from a found object or a convert's property.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב הוּנָא holds that מוּסִירָה works for acquisition from a person but not for a found object or a convert's property. This distinction is crucial for understanding the mechanics of acquisition.

phrases.aramaic: אִתְּמַר נָמֵי, אָמַר רַבִּי חֶלְבּוֹ אָמַר רַב הוּנָא:
phrases.english: It was also stated, Rabbi Helbo said Rav Huna said:
Holding the reins from his friend – he acquired.
In a found object and in a convert's property – he does not acquire.

steps.triggerLanguage: אמר רבי חלבו אמר רב הונא: מוסירה מחברו – קנה

rashi.hebrew: מחבירו - מקח וממכר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that מוסירה from a friend refers to a standard מקח וממכר transaction.

rashi.hebrew: בנכסי הגר - שמת ואין לו יורשין וכל הקודם בנכסיו זכה בהן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that נכסי הגר refers to the property of a convert who died without heirs, where the first to seize the assets acquires them.

steps.title: Clarifying the meaning of מוסירה
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks for the definition of מוסירה and Rava explains it as a transfer mechanism.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא seeks to understand the term מוּסִירָה and רַבָּא provides an explanation. He explains that מוּסִירָה is like a person who transmits an item to another.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבָּא explains that מוּסִירָה works for acquisition from a person because there is a transfer, but not for a found object or convert's property because there is no transferor. This clarifies the mechanics of acquisition by מוּסִירָה.

phrases.english: What is the language of reins? Rava said:

phrases.aramaic: אִידִי אַסְבְּרָא לִי כְּאָדָם הַמּוֹסֵר דָּבָר לַחֲבֵירוֹ.
phrases.english: Idi explained to me like a person who transmits a thing to his friend.

phrases.aramaic: בִּשְׁלָמָא מֵחֲבֵירוֹ קָנֵי, דְּקָא מָסַר לֵיהּ חַבְרֵיהּ.
phrases.english: Granted, from his friend he acquires, as his friend transmits to him.

phrases.aramaic: אֶלָּא בִּמְצִיאָה וּבְנִכְסֵי הַגֵּר מַאן קָא
phrases.english: But in a find and in the property of a convert, who is
transmitting to him that he should acquire?

steps.triggerLanguage: מאי לשון מוסירה? אמר רבה: אידי אסברא לי כאדם המוסר דבר לחברו

rashi.hebrew: ומהו לשון מוסירה - שמוסרין אותו בו ללוקח:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the term מוסירה refers to the act of transferring an item to the buyer.

steps.title: Challenge from the Mishnah about two riders
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the previous conclusions with a Mishnah about two riders.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises an objection from a מִשְׁנָה that seems to contradict the previous conclusions. The מִשְׁנָה states that two riders divide the animal, which implies a rider acquires.
steps.deeperAnalysis: If the מִשְׁנָה is according to רַבִּי מֵאִר, who holds that even one in a wagon acquires, then a rider certainly acquires. If it's according to the רַבָּנִים, who exempt one in a wagon, then a rider should not acquire. The גמרא concludes that the מִשְׁנָה must be according to the רַבָּנִים, and thus a rider does acquire.

phrases.aramaic: מֵיתִיבִי: הָיוּ שְׁנַיִם רוֹכְבִין עַל גַּבֵּי בְּהֵמָה וְכוּ׳.
phrases.english: He sat: Were two riding on an animal, etc.
Whose is it? If we say Rabbi Meir –
Now, one sitting acquires,
does one riding need to be stated?!

phrases.aramaic: אֶלָּא לָאו רַבָּנַן, וּשְׁמַע מִינַּהּ רָכוּב קָנֵי.
phrases.english: Rather, is it not the Rabbis, and learn from it that one riding acquires.

steps.triggerLanguage: מיתיבי: היו שנים רוכבין על גבי בהמה וכו׳

rashi.hebrew: אי נימא ר' מאיר - דאמר היושב בקרון סופג את הארבעים ומתניתין יחידאה היא ולית הלכתא כוותיה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara cannot attribute the Mishnah to רבי מאיר because he is a יחידאה and הלכה is not like him.

rashi.hebrew: השתא לר"מ יושב קני - דקאמר היושב בקרון סופג את הארבעים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that רבי מאיר holds that a person sitting in a wagon acquires, as derived from his ruling that such a person is liable for lashes.

rashi.hebrew: רכוב מיבעיא - ולמה לי לאשמועינן בתרתי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that if the Mishnah were according to רבי מאיר, it would be redundant to teach about a rider since a rider's acquisition is even more obvious than a sitter's.

rashi.hebrew: אלא לאו רבנן - ואשמועינן דביושב הוא דפטור אבל רכוב לא ותיובתא לרב יהודה דפשט רוכב מיושב:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י concludes that the Mishnah must be according to the רבנן, who exempt a sitter but not a rider, thereby serving as a תיובתא to רב יהודה who derived a rider's status from a sitter's.

steps.title: Resolution: The rider is also leading
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the challenge by stating that the rider in the Mishnah is also leading the animal.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא resolves the challenge by reinterpreting the מִשְׁנָה's scenario. It clarifies that the rider is actively leading the animal with his legs.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The rider is not passive; he is an active leader. Acquisition occurs through leading, not merely riding. This distinction resolves the apparent contradiction.

phrases.english: Here, with what are we dealing? With one who leads with his legs.
If so, this is leading!
Two types of leading,
What is it that you say: One who rides is preferable,
For behold, he leads and also holds it,
He is teaching us.

rashi.hebrew: במנהיג ברגליו - דקנייה במשיכה שבועט בה ברגליו והולכת מחמתו כדרך רוכבי סוסים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that מנהיג ברגליו means the rider acquires the animal through משיכה by kicking it with his legs, causing it to move as is customary for horse riders.

rashi.hebrew: היינו מנהיג - והו"ל למיתני או שהיו שניהם מנהיגים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י notes that if the Mishnah meant a simple leader, it should have simply stated that both were leaders, implying the need to distinguish between types of leadership.

rashi.hebrew: תרי גווני מנהיג - דאי אשמועינן בשני מנהיגים דיחלוקו לא היינו יודעים להאי מהו דתימא כו':
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the Gemara teaches two types of מנהיג because if we only knew that two simple leaders split the item, we might have assumed a rider is superior and acquires the whole item.

tosafot.hebrew: מהו דתימא רכוב עדיף - פי' חזקתו עדיף ויבטל את חזקת המנהיג קמ"ל דשניהם מוחזקים ויחלוקו אבל אין לפרש מהו דתימא דרכוב עדיף ויבטל את קנין המנהיג קמ"ל דשניהם קנו כדפרישית לעיל דאפילו לא הוי קני במקום רכוב מקרי מוחזק כיון דמנהיג לחודיה קני וא"ת לעיל כי פריך השתא יושב קני רכוב מיבעיא לישני דקא משמע לן דלא מבטל חזקת רכוב דתפיס בה ואזלא מחמתיה את חזקת המנהיג וי"ל דאין צריך להשמיענו דפשיטא דרכוב לחודיה בלא מנהיג ברגליו לא חשיב לבטל את חזקת המנהיג דאי משום דתפיס בה מנהיג נמי אזלא מחמתיה טפי מרכוב אבל השתא דמיירי במנהיג ברגליו דכיון דכל מה שיש במנהיג יש ברכוב ועוד דתפיס בה יבטל את חזקת המנהיג קא משמע לן ולפי מאי דאוקימנא השתא במנהיג ברגליו אתיא כרבנן וכל שכן כרבי מאיר:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that מהו דתימא רכוב עדיף refers to the rider's חזקה being superior and nullifying the leader's חזקה, teaching that both are מוחזקים and split the item, rather than discussing קניין directly.

steps.title: Challenge from a בְּרֵיתָא about pulling and leading
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara raises a challenge from a Baraita about pulling and leading.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a challenge from a בְּרֵיתָא that omits the rider. This omission suggests a rider does not acquire, contradicting the previous resolution.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The בְּרֵיתָא lists pulling and leading as acquisition methods. It excludes riding, implying riding alone is insufficient. This challenges the rider-leader resolution.

phrases.aramaic: תָּא שְׁמַע: שְׁנַיִם שֶׁהָיוּ מוֹשְׁכִין בְּגָמָל, וּמַנְהִיגִין בַּחֲמוֹר,
phrases.english: Come and hear: Two who were pulling a camel, and leading a donkey,

phrases.aramaic: אוֹ שֶׁהָיָה אֶחָד מוֹשֵׁךְ וְאֶחָד מַנְהִיג,
phrases.english: or one was pulling and one was leading,
by this measure they acquired.
Rabbi Yehuda says:

phrases.aramaic: לְעוֹלָם לֹא קָנָה עַד שֶׁתְּהֵא מְשִׁיכָה
phrases.english: Forever he did not acquire until it be pulling
in a camel and leading in a donkey. They say nevertheless:

phrases.aramaic: אוֹ שֶׁהָיָה אֶחָד מוֹשֵׁךְ וְאֶחָד מַנְהִיג,
phrases.english: or one was pulling and one was leading,
pulling and leading — yes.
But riding — no!

steps.triggerLanguage: תא שמע: שנים שהיו מושכין בגמל, ומנהיגין בחמור

rashi.hebrew: חמור דרכו בהנהגה וגמל דרכו במשיכה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the distinction between pulling a camel and leading a donkey is based on the customary method of moving each animal, which determines the valid קניין.
